frontmatter.title: A Wild Universal Cone From a Splitting of the Generic Element

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a wild category, and let \(I:\mathbf{ob}_{\mathcal{C}}\) be an object of \(\mathcal{C}\) such that \({\Delta I}\xrightarrow{\alpha}{1_{\mathcal{C}}}\) is a wild natural transformation from the constant 0-functor \({\mathcal{C}}\xrightarrow{\Delta I}{\mathcal{C}}\) to the identity 0-functor \({\mathcal{C}}\xrightarrow{1_{\mathcal{C}}}{\mathcal{C}}\) on \(\mathcal{C}\) for some \(I:\mathbf{ob}_{\mathcal{C}}\). Now let \(r:\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(I,J\right)}\) and \(s:\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(J,I\right)}\) be a section-retraction pair witnessed by \(\eta : s;r = 1_{I}\), and let \(\sigma : r;s=\alpha_{I}\) be a splitting of \(\alpha_{I}:\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(I,I\right)}\). Then we may define a wild natural transformation \({\Delta J}\xrightarrow{\beta}{1_{\mathcal{C}}}\) and an identification \(\chi : \beta_{J} = 1_{J}\).
(This construction is due to Mike Shulman.)
mainmatter.block:
Proof.
Define the component \(\beta_{A}:\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(J,A\right)}\) to be the composite \(s;\alpha_{A}\); for the wild naturality structure, we fix \(f:\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(A,B\right)}\) to define \(\beta_{f} : \beta_{A};f = 1_{J};\beta_{B}\) as follows: \[\begin{aligned} \beta_{A};f & = {\left(s;\alpha_{A}\right)};f \\ & = s;{\left(1_{A};\alpha_{B}\right)} \\ & = s;\alpha_{B} \\ & = 1_{J}; {\left(s;\alpha_{B}\right)} \\ & = 1_{J}; \beta_{B} \end{aligned} \]
Next we identify \(\beta_{J}\) with \(1_{J}\). By incoherent naturality, we have \(\alpha_{J};s = \alpha_{I}\); thus by our splitting of \(\alpha_{I}\) we have \(\alpha_{J};s = r;s\) and hence by postcomposing \(r\) and canceling via \(\eta:s;r=1_{I}\) we conclude that \(\alpha_J = r\). Therefore \(\beta_J=s;\alpha_J=s;r=1_{J}\).

frontmatter.title: Wild Initial Objects From Universal Cones

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a wild category, and let \(I:\mathbf{ob}_{\mathcal{C}}\) be an object of \(\mathcal{C}\). If we may equip a wild natural transformation \({\Delta I}\xrightarrow{\alpha}{1_{\mathcal{C}}}\) from the constant 0-functor \({\mathcal{C}}\xrightarrow{\Delta I}{\mathcal{C}}\) to the identity 0-functor \({\mathcal{C}}\xrightarrow{1_{\mathcal{C}}}{\mathcal{C}}\) with an identification \(c_\alpha : \alpha_I = 1_{I}\), then \(\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(I,A\right)}\) is contractible for all \(A:\mathbf{ob}_{\mathcal{C}}\).

(Our proof will follow that of the [nLab](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/initial+object#cone), which was stated for ordinary categories and functors and natural transformations rather than wild ones. It was Mike Shulman who noticed who noticed that this proof also works for the wild/incoherent notions studied here.)

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: To show that \(\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(I,A\right)}\) is contractible, we choose \(\alpha_{A} : \mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(I,A\right)}\) as the center of contraction. To identify any \(f : \mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(I,A\right)}\) with \(\alpha_{A}\), we use the wild naturality of \({\Delta I}\xrightarrow{\alpha}{1_{\mathcal{C}}}\). In particular, we have an identification \(\alpha_f\) between \(\alpha_I;f\) and \(1_{I};\alpha_A\). By our assumption that \(\alpha_I=1_{I}\) and using the wild left-unit identifications \(\lambda f\) and \(\lambda \alpha_A\), we may identify \(f\) with \(\alpha_A\).

frontmatter.title: A Wild Universal Cone From a Splitting of the Generic Element

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a wild category, and let \(I:\mathbf{ob}_{\mathcal{C}}\) be an object of \(\mathcal{C}\) such that \({\Delta I}\xrightarrow{\alpha}{1_{\mathcal{C}}}\) is a wild natural transformation from the constant 0-functor \({\mathcal{C}}\xrightarrow{\Delta I}{\mathcal{C}}\) to the identity 0-functor \({\mathcal{C}}\xrightarrow{1_{\mathcal{C}}}{\mathcal{C}}\) on \(\mathcal{C}\) for some \(I:\mathbf{ob}_{\mathcal{C}}\). Now let \(r:\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(I,J\right)}\) and \(s:\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(J,I\right)}\) be a section-retraction pair witnessed by \(\eta : s;r = 1_{I}\), and let \(\sigma : r;s=\alpha_{I}\) be a splitting of \(\alpha_{I}:\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(I,I\right)}\). Then we may define a wild natural transformation \({\Delta J}\xrightarrow{\beta}{1_{\mathcal{C}}}\) and an identification \(\chi : \beta_{J} = 1_{J}\).
(This construction is due to Mike Shulman.)
mainmatter.block:
Proof.
Define the component \(\beta_{A}:\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(J,A\right)}\) to be the composite \(s;\alpha_{A}\); for the wild naturality structure, we fix \(f:\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(A,B\right)}\) to define \(\beta_{f} : \beta_{A};f = 1_{J};\beta_{B}\) as follows: \[\begin{aligned} \beta_{A};f & = {\left(s;\alpha_{A}\right)};f \\ & = s;{\left(1_{A};\alpha_{B}\right)} \\ & = s;\alpha_{B} \\ & = 1_{J}; {\left(s;\alpha_{B}\right)} \\ & = 1_{J}; \beta_{B} \end{aligned} \]
Next we identify \(\beta_{J}\) with \(1_{J}\). By incoherent naturality, we have \(\alpha_{J};s = \alpha_{I}\); thus by our splitting of \(\alpha_{I}\) we have \(\alpha_{J};s = r;s\) and hence by postcomposing \(r\) and canceling via \(\eta:s;r=1_{I}\) we conclude that \(\alpha_J = r\). Therefore \(\beta_J=s;\alpha_J=s;r=1_{J}\).

tree.mainmatter:
Let \(\mathcal{C}\) and \(\mathcal{D}\) be wild categories, and let \(D:\mathbf{ob}_{\mathcal{D}}\) be an object of \(\mathcal{D}\). We define the constant 0-functor on \(D:\mathbf{ob}_{\mathcal{D}}\) out of \(\mathcal{C}\) to be the 0-functor \({\mathcal{C}}\xrightarrow{\Delta D}{\mathcal{D}}\) defined as follows:\[\begin{aligned} \mathbf{ob}_{\Delta D}C & :\equiv D \\ \mathbf{hom}_{\Delta D} f & :\equiv 1_{D}\end{aligned}\]
The above may be extended to a wild 1-semi-functor as follows:\[\mathsf{seq}_{F}\,f\,g :\equiv \mathsf{refl}\]

Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a wild category; the identity 0-functor \({\mathcal{C}}\xrightarrow{1_{\mathcal{C}}}{\mathcal{C}}\) on \(\mathcal{C}\) is defined as follows:\[\begin{aligned} \mathbf{ob}_{1_{\mathcal{C}}}A & :\equiv A \\ \mathbf{hom}_{1_{\mathcal{C}}}f & :\equiv f \end{aligned}\]
The above may be extended to a wild 1-semi-functor as follows:\[ \mathsf{seq}_{F}\,f\,g :\equiv \mathsf{refl}\]

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{D}\) be two wild categories, and let \(F,G:\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}\) be two wild 0-functors from \(\mathcal{C}\) to \(\mathcal{D}\). A wild natural transformation is defined by the following data:
A wild category is given by the following data:

ol.li: for each \(A,B:\mathbf{ob}_{\mathcal{C}}\), a family of types \(\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(A,B\right)}\);

for each \(f:\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(A,B\right)}\), a identification \(\mathsf{lunit}_{\mathcal{C}} f : {\left(1_{A};f\right)} = f\) and a identification \(\mathsf{runit}_{\mathcal{C}} f : {\left(f;1_{B}\right)}=f\);

for each \(f:\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(A,B\right)}\) and \(g:\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(B,C\right)}\) and \(h:\mathbf{hom}_{\mathcal{C}}{\left(C,D\right)}\), a identification \(\mathsf{assoc}_{\mathcal{C}}\,f\,g\,h : f;{\left(g;h\right)} = {\left(f;g\right)};h\).

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{U}\in\mathcal{V}\) be an impredicative pair of universes with \(A:\mathcal{V}\). There we may construct a reflection algebra \(A_{\mathcal{U}} :{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) such that for any other reflection algebra \(X:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), the space of homomorphisms \(A_{\mathcal{U}}\multimap X\) is contractible.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Our splitting of the generic element of the generic cone \({\Delta I}\xrightarrow{p}{1_{{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}}\) over the identity functor on reflection algebras yields a new (incoherent) cone \({\Delta J}\xrightarrow{q}{1_{{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}}\) whose generic element \(q_{J}:J\multimap J\) may be identified with the identity homomorphism. It follows that each space of homomorphisms \(J\to X\) is contractible. Therefore, we define \(A_{\mathcal{U}}\) to be the apex \(J\).
